title: Preach
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro

outline.title: Introduction: Why Do We Have Preaching?
subpoints: Preaching might seem outdated — why not drama, choreography, or other media?
Answer: because God speaks through preaching; in creation and redemption, God spoke
God chose the spoken Word as the center of the church's life — he knew every technology that would come and still appointed preaching
God illustrated how he brings life to dead people: Ezekiel in the valley of dry bones was told to speak to the dead, and they came alive — preaching is speaking to dying people

outline.title: 1. The Bible Is God Speaking
subpoints: 2 Timothy 3:16: theopneustos — God-breathed; God spoke every word from Genesis 1 to Revelation 22
Not only the concepts but the very words: multiple NT arguments rest on a single letter of an OT word (e.g., 'seed' not 'seeds' in Galatians 3)
When we preach the Bible, God is speaking to us; the Reformers understood this — grace is dispensed especially through the preaching of the Word
The Bible commends gathering to hear the Word preached, not merely having daily devotions at home; what happens in corporate worship is unique

outline.title: 2. The Command to Preach
subpoints: 2 Timothy 4:1: Paul charges Timothy under oath — before God and Christ who will judge the living and the dead — to preach the Word
Timothy appears to have had a tendency toward timidity and fear of men; Paul's answer: your judge is God, not the congregation
'Be ready in season and out of season' — when it's convenient (people want what you have) and when it's inconvenient (people don't want to hear it)
In that preaching: convince, rebuke, exhort — with all patience and teaching

outline.title: 3. The Congregation's Responsibility
subpoints: 2 Timothy 4:3-4: 'the time will come when they will not endure sound doctrine' — this refers to professing Christians in the church, not 'the bad world out there'
People with itching ears heap up for themselves teachers who say what they want to hear — this happens because congregations allow it and want it
The congregation of Christ bears responsibility to ensure the preaching from the pulpit is from the Word of God; ear-scratching preaching only happens because people demand it
History confirms this: Congregationalists becoming Unitarians, mainline denominations going liberal in the late 1800s — people turned to fables

outline.title: 4. The Pastor as Preacher
subpoints: 2 Timothy 4:5: 'do the work of an evangelist, fulfill your ministry' — a faithful preacher will face afflictions
A pastor is more than a preacher — shepherding, counseling, and care are all part of it
But a pastor is not less than a preacher; one of the greatest ways a pastor can love his congregation is by faithfully proclaiming the Word of God
Prayer: that this would be a congregation that desires the preaching of the Word, the exposition of Scripture, the proclamation of the gospel — in season and out

summary: A PM sermon on 2 Timothy 3:16–4:5 addressing why the church has preaching. Pastor Tito Lyro argues that God speaks through preaching — as he spoke creation into existence and brought the valley of dry bones to life, he has appointed the preaching of his Word as the center of the church's life. The Bible is God's direct speech (theopneustos — breathed out), including the very words, not just the concepts. Timothy is charged under solemn oath to preach in season and out of season, because a time is coming when people will not endure sound doctrine. Soberly, the congregation shares responsibility: ear-scratching preaching happens because congregations allow it and demand it. A pastor is more than a preacher, but never less — faithful proclamation is one of the primary ways a pastor loves his people.
transcript:
## Introduction: Why Do We Have Preaching?

For our scripture reading this afternoon, let's turn to the second epistle of Paul to Timothy, chapter 3, starting at verse 16, reading through verse 5 of chapter 4.

*All Scripture is given by inspiration of God and is profitable for doctrine, for reproof, for correction, for instruction in righteousness, that the man of God may be complete, thoroughly equipped for every good work. I charge you therefore before God and the Lord Jesus Christ, who will judge the living and the dead at His appearing and His kingdom: Preach the word! Be ready in season and out of season. Convince, rebuke, exhort, with all longsuffering and teaching. For the time will come when they will not endure sound doctrine, but according to their own desires, because they have itching ears, they will heap up for themselves teachers; and they will turn their ears away from the truth, and be turned aside to fables. But you be watchful in all things, endure afflictions, do the work of an evangelist, fulfill your ministry.*

We have grown used to having preaching in our church — that seems to be how things have always been, and sometimes we may not even question why. Why do we have preaching as part of our worship service? Why have a guy up front speaking? Why not something more dynamic — drama, perhaps some choreography — why not do it differently?

The answer is that God speaks through preaching. That is why.

In creation and in redemption, God speaks. He spoke creation into existence. Paul grabs onto that in 2 Corinthians and says that God also redeemed his people by the power of his word. Throughout the Old Testament we have the testimony of God speaking: *Thus says the Lord.* It is the spoken word that is used to minister to God's people.

And God illustrated how he brings people to life. He took Ezekiel to a valley of dry bones and told him to speak to those bones. Preaching, as someone has said, is a dying man preaching to dying people. God instituted it because he appointed the speaking of his word to be the center of the life of his church — then and now.

God knew what technologies were going to develop. He knew what media would be available a thousand years in the future if the Lord doesn't return. He knows all that — and he still appointed the preaching of his word. We cannot out-do God when he appoints the preaching of his word.

## 1. The Bible Is God Speaking

Why does God appoint preaching? Because the Bible itself is God speaking. When verse 16 says *all Scripture is given by inspiration of God* — that word *inspiration*, in Greek, means *breathed out*. Breathing out is speaking. God spoke every word that we have in our Bible. From Genesis 1 to Revelation 22, it is God speaking. Not only the concepts — but also the very words used.

Several times, arguments are made in the New Testament based on a single letter of an Old Testament word. When Paul says in Galatians that God did not say *seeds* but *seed* — just the difference of a plural and a singular — he builds a whole theological argument on that. So the very words we have are spoken by God. When we preach the Bible, the act of preaching itself is God speaking to us.

The Reformers understood that. Our confession says that it is in the reading of the Word, and especially in the preaching of the Word, that grace is dispensed — grace made available to God's people.

You may know that nowhere does the Bible commend daily devotions. I dare you to find a text that says that. But the Bible does commend being in the Lord's house to hear the preaching of the Word of God. I am not saying you should not read your Bible at home. But I want you to think more highly of what we are doing on the Lord's Day, when the Word of God is proclaimed. The Spirit uses that Word in a way that is different from when you are reading your Bible alone.

## 2. The Command to Preach

In chapter 4, verse 1, Paul puts Timothy under solemn oath: *I charge you therefore before God and the Lord Jesus Christ, who will judge the living and the dead at His appearing and His kingdom: Preach the word.*

Timothy seems to have been a timid man — perhaps afraid of what people might think, perhaps prone to softening what needed to be said. Paul's answer: Timothy, are you afraid of what people might think of you? Guess what — your judge is going to be God. He is the one at the end of time who will judge you for what you preached. So preach the Word.

Not just preach — preach *the word*. Be ready in season and out of season. Someone once said there are only two times a pastor should preach: when he's in season and when he's out of season. In season means when it's convenient, when people want what you have to give. Out of season means when it's inconvenient, when people don't want to hear what you have to say. Paul commands every pastor, in the name of Christ, to preach the Word at both times.

In that preaching: *convince, rebuke, exhort, with all longsuffering and teaching.* Why? Because a time is coming when people will not want that.

## 3. The Congregation's Responsibility

*For the time will come when they will not endure sound doctrine, but according to their own desires, because they have itching ears, they will heap up for themselves teachers; and they will turn their ears away from the truth, and be turned aside to fables.*

This is talking about professing Christians in the church — not the bad world out there. These are people in the pew. Look at the responsibility of the congregation: *they will heap up for themselves teachers.* It is the people with itching ears who cause the problem. Ear-scratching preaching happens because congregations allow it and want it.

It is on your shoulders, congregation of the Lord Jesus Christ, to ensure that the preaching from this pulpit is from the Word of God, according to Jesus Christ. When preaching becomes nothing more than scratching itching ears, it happens because the congregation wanted it. They allowed it.

History confirms this time and again: the Congregationalists becoming Unitarians, the mainline denominations going liberal in the late 1800s — people turned to fables. And notice: what the Bible teaches is what is true. The mythical things are the ideas that Jesus is not God, that God doesn't save, that the Bible is not trustworthy. Those are the fables.

## 4. The Pastor as Preacher

*But you be watchful in all things, endure afflictions, do the work of an evangelist, fulfill your ministry.*

If you preach Jesus and the gospel of Jesus Christ, at some point in your ministry you will face afflictions. That is part of fulfilling the ministry.

A pastor is more than a preacher. A man who only stands and preaches is a preacher — and there is room for that. But a pastor is not *less* than a preacher. One of the greatest ways that a pastor can love his congregation is by being ready to proclaim the Word of God. Shepherding the congregation is essential to being a pastor. But faithful proclamation of the Word is a fundamental, essential element.

My prayer — as it has been for the past six years, and my prayer for the next six years — is that this would be a congregation that desires the milk of the Word of God, a congregation zealous for the proclamation of the Word of God. Because as we do that, we are going to see Christ more and more every time we gather. And the darker the world becomes, the brighter our light will shine as we proclaim the gospel — in season and out of season, in every season — as a church that does not waver from the Word of God.

Father, thank you that you are good to us. Thank you for your Word, and thank you that we can have it preached to us. Continue to bless the preaching of your Word in this church and in every church that names the name of Jesus. We pray that you would revive churches that refuse to preach your Word. Bring them back to the Scriptures, and let us see a great revival of solid preaching in this area, this country, and all over the world. In Jesus' name, amen.
